original_abstract: . We introduce a new characterization of the NP complexity class, called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), which is a natural exten- sion of span programs deﬁned by Karchmer and Wigderson. Our main motivation is the quick construction of succinct, easily veriﬁed arguments for NP statements. To achieve this goal, QSPs use a new approach to the well-known technique of arithmetization of Boolean circuits. Our new approach yields dramatic performance improvements. Using QSPs, we construct a NIZK argument – in the CRS model – for Circuit-SAT consisting of just 7 group elements. The CRS size and prover computation are quasi-linear, making our scheme seemingly quite practical, a result supported by our implementation. Indeed, our NIZK argument attains the shortest proof, most eﬃcient prover, and most eﬃcient veriﬁer of any known technique. We also present a variant of QSPs, called Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs), that “naturally” compute arithmetic circuits over large ﬁelds, along with succinct NIZK constructions that use QAPs. Finally, we show how QSPs and QAPs can be used to eﬃciently and publicly verify outsourced computations, where a client asks a server to compute F(x) for a given function F and must verify the result provided by the server in considerably less time than it would take to compute F from scratch. The resulting schemes are the most eﬃcient, general- purpose publicly veriﬁable computation schemes. ⋆The research of this author was sponsored by the U.S. Army Research Laboratory and the U.K. Ministry of Defence and was accomplished under Agreement Number W911NF-06-3-0001. The views and conclusions contained in this document are those of the author(s) and should not be interpreted as representing the oﬃcial policies, either expressed or implied, of the U.S. Army Research Laboratory, the U.S. Government, the U.K. Ministry of Defence or the U.K. Government. The U.S. and U.K. Governments are authorized to reproduce and distribute reprints for Government purp
humanized_abstract: . In this paper we introduce a new characterization of the NP complexity class called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs) that is a natural extension of span programs introduced by Karchmer and Wigderson. Our motivation for introducing the class of QSPs is in order to design very quick protocols for the construction of “evidence” or “proofs” in the form of NIZK (non-interactive zero-knowledge) arguments for NP statements. Toward this end, our approach to the arithmetization of Boolean circuits is significantly diﬀerent from earlier approaches. Our approach allows for very dramatic improvements in the resulting constructions. Our main result here is a NIZK argument for Circuit-SAT in the common reference string (CRS) model based on QSPs that uses only 7 group elements. In addition, both the CRS length and the prover’s computation is quasi-linear, which is quite fast in practice (as we confirm with experimental results). The resulting NIZK argument has the shortest length of proofs that we know of, fastest prover and fastest verifier for Circuit-SAT. We also introduce a simple extension of the class of QSPs called Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs) that gives us a convenient way of describing arithmetic circuits over large fields. In addition, we obtain a new family of NIZK proof protocols that are based on these circuits and are very simple to prove correct, while still remaining eﬃcient. We use both our characterizations of NP in order to design very eﬃcient public protocols for publicly verifiable computation. Specifically, we show that QSPs and the QAPs can be used in order to publicly verify that a server that is given some input F has computed the output of an arbitrary polynomial time function of that input. That is, for any (possibly randomized) function F of an input x and for any polynomial time prover that receives the output of F as input, our server protocol allows for the verification of the correctness of the computation performed on F by the server in constant time which is less than the time required in order to evaluate F given the input x. In addition, the server’s computation time is constant which is also an improvement compared to the required time in order to evaluate F.
